title: When Big O Doesn't Matter
subtitle: The advanced topic that interviewers rarely teach

sections.heading: Big O is a tool, not a religion
sections.body:
Engineers who've only studied for interviews can become Big-O zealots: 'this is O(n²), it's bad'. In real code, that's often wrong.

Big O is **asymptotic** — it tells you what happens as inputs get huge. For small inputs, constants and cache behavior can dominate completely. An O(n²) algorithm with small constants can beat an O(n log n) algorithm with large constants — for the actual inputs you care about.

sections.heading: Reason 1: small n
sections.body: If n is bounded — say, you're processing a config file with ≤100 entries — your algorithm runs in essentially constant time anyway. Picking O(n) over O(n²) saves nothing perceptible. Pick whichever code is cleaner.

sections.heading: Reason 2: constants matter at human scales
sections.body:
Big O drops constant factors. In the real world, constants come from:

* **Cache locality**: arrays beat linked lists by ~10× for the same Big O
* **Branch prediction**: predictable loops beat unpredictable ones
* **Memory allocation**: avoiding `new` calls in a hot loop matters
* **System calls**: a single disk read costs ~100,000 in-memory operations

An O(n log n) algorithm with bad cache behavior can lose to an O(n²) one with great locality, for n in the tens of thousands.

sections.heading: Reason 3: the wrong thing to measure
sections.body:
Sometimes time complexity isn't the bottleneck at all.

* Your app makes 5 API calls. The network costs 50ms each. Whether your inner loop is O(n) or O(n log n) is invisible next to the 250ms of waiting.
* You're processing 10 GB of data. The disk read dominates. Your O(n²) sort is fine if 'n' is the number of *files* (say, 100).
* You're rendering a UI 60 times per second. Layout cost matters; sort cost almost never.

Measure where time actually goes. **Profile, don't predict.**

sections.heading: Reason 4: readable code wins on small budgets
sections.body: If two implementations are both fast enough, pick the one your team will understand at 2am. A clever O(log n) approach using a custom segment tree is worse than a simple O(n) loop when n ≤ 100 and you have to maintain it for years.

sections.heading: When Big O DOES matter (most of the time)
sections.body:
Don't take this lesson as 'ignore Big O'. It matters when:

* **n could be huge** — anywhere user input or data size can grow unbounded
* **You're inside a hot loop** — code that runs many times per request, or in a tight render loop
* **You're choosing data structures** — array vs linked list vs hash map vs tree decisions are best made by complexity
* **You're scaling up** — what worked on 1000 rows breaks on 1,000,000

The job is to know when each consideration applies. Big O is the *starting point* — it tells you what's possible. Then profile, measure, and optimize what you find.

sections.caption: When to actually care about Big O

data.text:
Situation                          | Does Big O matter?
  -----------------------------------+-------------------
  Bounded small input (n ≤ 100)      | almost never
  User-facing list of <1000 items    | rarely
  Backend processing user data       | yes
  Database query against a big table | absolutely
  Inside a per-frame render loop     | yes
  One-shot startup code              | only if it's slow
  Network/IO-bound operation         | profile first
  Cryptography / hashing             | choose proven algos
  Library code reused everywhere     | yes, design for n→∞

questions.question: Which is the MOST important reason Big O can mislead?
options: It ignores constants and cache behavior
questions.explanation: Real performance depends on hidden constants — cache, branch prediction, memory allocation. Big O drops them all.

questions.question: True or false: if Algorithm A is O(n) and Algorithm B is O(n²), A is always faster.
questions.explanation: False — only for sufficiently large n. For small n, the constants in A might dwarf B's quadratic growth.

questions.question: When is Big O analysis MOST important?
options: When inputs are bounded and tiny
When inputs are user-controlled and can grow
When the code runs once at startup
questions.explanation: Unbounded growth is when bad Big O bites. Bounded tiny input rarely benefits from elaborate optimization.
